content.assessment: A pilot approach has an attractive fiscal profile: it limits initial exposure to a subset of regions before committing to national-scale restructuring, allowing cost-effectiveness to be tested empirically rather than assumed, consistent with cross-national meta-analytic evidence showing no significant efficiency losses from de-tracking. However, the finance lens flags that without paired zoning/choice controls, the pilot may misestimate true implementation costs and benefits, since choice-driven segregation could reassert itself independent of the tracking variable being tested.
opportunities: Pairing with zoning/choice controls (T4) could produce a more robust joint test of combined intervention cost-effectiveness
Piloting allows phased fiscal commitment, reducing risk of large sunk costs if results are unfavorable
strengths: Limits fiscal and political exposure by testing on a regional scale before committing to national resource reallocation
Cross-national meta-analytic evidence of no significant efficiency losses from de-tracking supports a favorable expected cost-benefit profile
threats: Poland's post-reform reversal despite measured success suggests even a successful, well-evaluated pilot may not secure durable fiscal/political commitment for scale-up
Finnish experience shows de-tracking alone is insufficient against choice-driven segregation, so a pilot without guardrails risks an incomplete and potentially misleading cost-benefit signal
weaknesses: Confounded historical comparators (Finland's simultaneous reforms) make isolating Hungary-specific cost-effectiveness difficult even with a pilot design
Volunteering pilot regions may not be representative, weakening the external validity of any value-for-money conclusions drawn
